name: Garden tomato (food medium)
basis: per 100 g fresh weight (raw, red, ripe fruit)

components.name: D-Glucose
components.source: USDA FDC 170457 (Tomatoes, red, ripe, raw, year round average)
components.evidence: Reducing hexose; co-dominant free sugar with fructose. Major fermentable carbon source.

components.name: D-Fructose
components.evidence: Slightly exceeds glucose in ripe fruit (Bapat/de Sousa 2018 PMC6046607). Major fermentable carbon source.

components.name: Sucrose
components.evidence: Negligible in ripe cultivated tomato; the fruit is a hexose (glucose+fructose) accumulator, sucrose is hydrolysed during ripening.

components.name: Starch
components.source: de Sousa et al. 2018 (PMC6046607); Davies & Cocking JSFA 1972
components.evidence: High in green fruit but almost fully converted to soluble sugars by the red-ripe stage; trace residual.

components.name: Citric acid
components.source: de Sousa et al. 2018 (PMC6046607); Davies & Cocking JSFA 1972 doi:10.1002/jsfa.2740230808
components.evidence: DOMINANT organic acid in red-ripe tomato (typical range 0.2-0.6 g/100g FW); principal titratable acid and a major respiratory/carbon substrate. Midpoint of literature range.

components.name: Malic acid
components.source: Davies & Cocking JSFA 1972; de Sousa et al. 2018 (PMC6046607)
components.evidence: Second organic acid; falls during ripening so red fruit malic << citric (typical 0.05-0.15 g/100g FW).

components.name: L-Ascorbate (vitamin C)
components.evidence: Ascorbic acid; both a vitamin and a minor organic acid.

components.name: 4-Aminobutyrate (GABA)
components.evidence: Abundant free non-protein amino acid in ripe tomato (typical ~20-40 mg/100g FW); readily catabolised N/C source.

components.name: L-Glutamate
components.evidence: Most abundant amino acid (total protein-bound + free). Free glutamate ~0.10-0.25 g/100g accumulates during ripening (umami); prime N source.

components.name: L-Aspartate
components.evidence: Second most abundant amino acid; large free pool in ripe fruit.

components.name: L-Serine
components.evidence: USDA reports L-serine; original FooDB 'D-Serine' is a mis-stereochemistry artifact.

components.name: L-Valine
components.evidence: Added; missing from current profile.

components.name: L-Phenylalanine
components.evidence: Added; missing from current profile.

components.name: Total protein
components.evidence: Low-protein matrix; much of the amino-N is free pool (glutamate/aspartate/GABA).

components.name: Biotin (B7)
components.evidence: Trace; not in USDA panel, literature estimate ~1-2 mcg/100g.

components.name: Folate (B9)
components.evidence: Total folate (DFE).

components.name: Beta-Carotene
components.evidence: Provitamin-A carotenoid (tomato supplies vitamin A activity as carotenoids, not retinol).

components.name: Lycopene
components.evidence: Dominant red carotenoid; non-provitamin-A.

components.name: Potassium
components.evidence: Dominant cation.

components.name: Phosphate (as P)
components.evidence: P content; supplies phosphate.

components.name: Selenium
components.evidence: Below detection in year-round average.

components.name: Molybdate
components.evidence: Genuine trace micronutrient (Mo cofactor / nitrate reductase); present but unquantified.

components.name: Boron
components.evidence: Genuine plant micronutrient; kept as trace (moved out of the noise metals).

components.name: Nitrate
components.evidence: Tomato accumulates nitrate (typ. 1-20 mg/100g); inorganic N source. Present, variety/soil dependent.

components.name: Sulfate
components.evidence: Sulfur source, present.

components.name: Chloride
components.evidence: Present.

components.name: Linoleic acid (18:2 n-6)
components.evidence: Dominant fatty acid of the (very low) tomato lipid fraction.

components.name: Total lipid (fat)
components.evidence: Tomato is essentially a non-fatty matrix.

components.name: Gallic acid
components.evidence: Genuine minor phenolic; retained.

components.name: Quercetin
components.evidence: Genuine flavonol (mostly as rutin in skin); retained.

remove.name: Arsenate
remove.reason: toxic non-nutritive trace contaminant, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Lead
remove.reason: toxic trace contaminant, not a nutrient

remove.name: Co2+
remove.reason: ultratrace; cobalt not a measured tomato nutrient, FooDB noise

remove.name: Nickel
remove.reason: ultratrace contaminant, not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Fluoride
remove.reason: trace, not a microbial growth nutrient

remove.name: Iodine c
remove.reason: negligible in tomato; not a growth nutrient

remove.name: Retinol
remove.reason: animal-form vitamin A; tomato contains 0 retinol (provitamin-A supplied as carotenes) — implausible/mis-mapped

remove.name: Fe2+ mitochondria
remove.reason: duplicate/compartment-tagged iron entry; iron already captured as 'Iron'

remove.name: Iron (Fe3+)
remove.reason: duplicate iron speciation entry; folded into single 'Iron'

remove.name: Maltose C12H22O11
remove.reason: not a real free sugar of ripe tomato (hexose accumulator); FooDB artifact

remove.name: D-Serine
remove.reason: wrong stereochemistry; protein serine is L-serine (corrected)

remove.name: Osmium
remove.reason: non-nutritive rare metal, implausible, FooDB noise

remove.name: Palladium
remove.reason: non-nutritive rare metal, implausible, FooDB noise

remove.name: epsilon-Polylysine
remove.reason: microbial-fermentation food additive/preservative, not a natural tomato constituent; implausible

remove.name: (±)-erythro-Isoleucine
remove.reason: stereoisomer artifact of isoleucine, duplicate noise

remove.name: (R)-Oxypeucedanin
remove.reason: furanocoumarin of Apiaceae/citrus, biologically implausible in tomato; FooDB predicted

missing_added: Citric acid (dominant organic acid, was absent)
GABA / 4-aminobutyrate (abundant free amino acid, was absent)
Explicit organic-acid and free-amino-acid pools that drive growth
sources: USDA FoodData Central FDC 170457 (Tomatoes, red, ripe, raw, year round average; SR Legacy 11529)
de Sousa Ferreira et al. 2018, MethodsX / Heliyon 'Quantification of sugars and organic acids in tomato fruits' PMC6046607
Davies & Cocking 1972, J Sci Food Agric 23:969 doi:10.1002/jsfa.2740230808 (organic acids during ripening)
FooDB FOOD00171 (broad compound list; source of removed noise)
notes: Dominant carbon/energy sources are the two free hexoses glucose (~1.25 g/100g) + fructose (~1.37 g/100g) and the organic acid citric acid (~0.32 g/100g, the defining acid of tomato, formerly missing); malic acid is a minor secondary acid. Nitrogen is supplied largely by a large free amino-acid pool dominated by glutamate/aspartate plus GABA, not by bulk protein (only 0.88 g/100g). Values are per 100 g fresh red-ripe fruit; ~34 heavy/rare-metal and implausible FooDB entries removed as non-nutritive noise.
